title: Functions: One Definition, Many Call Sites
learningObjectives: Define a function with parameters and a return value instead of relying on duplicated inline logic
Explain the difference between printing a result and returning one
Refactor duplicated logic (the threshold classifier) into a single reusable function
Write a clearly named function whose purpose is obvious from its signature alone
whyItMatters: The threshold-classification logic has now been written twice — once outside a loop, once inside one. Every mission from here on reuses forecasting, optimisation, and routing logic dozens of times; functions are what stop the AI Energy OS from becoming an unmaintainable pile of duplicated logic.
capstoneContribution: The first reusable function in the Digital Twin's codebase — classify_demand() — called from every place that previously duplicated the threshold check inline.

problem.summary: The same classification logic now exists in two places and can silently drift out of sync.
problem.content: Your threshold-classification logic now exists twice: once outside a loop, once inside one. If the safety limit changes, you have to remember to update it in both places — and eventually you won't.

motivation.summary: Duplicated logic quietly drifting out of sync is a real, common engineering failure mode.
motivation.content: This is the exact failure mode real engineering teams spend years fighting: logic duplicated in multiple places quietly drifting out of sync. A function collapses every copy into one definition with one place to fix.

theory.summary: def creates a reusable block; return hands a value back to the caller, unlike print().
theory.content: `def classify_demand(reading_kw, safe_limit_kw=5000): ... return label` defines a reusable block. Parameters let the caller supply different inputs each time; `return` sends a value back to the caller, distinct from `print()`, which only displays it and gives nothing back to the rest of the program. Default parameter values (`safe_limit_kw=5000`) let common cases omit the argument entirely.

visualExplanation.summary: Two duplicated logic blocks collapse into one shared block with inputs in and a labeled output.
visualExplanation.content: The diagram shows the classification logic pulled out of both call sites (the standalone check and the loop) into one box with inputs going in and a labeled output value coming out, with two arrows pointing back into where it's called from.

interactiveDemo.summary: Rewire two duplicated blocks into calls to one shared function block.
interactiveDemo.content: Rewire two duplicated logic blocks in a visual editor into calls to a single shared function block, watching both call sites update simultaneously when the shared block's logic changes.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: extract the classification logic into a function with defaults.

independentCoding.summary: Write classify_battery() and call it in a loop, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Write a function `classify_battery(soc_percent)` that returns `CRITICAL`, `LOW`, or `OK` per the thresholds from the Control Flow lesson, then call it in a loop over a list of readings.

debugging.summary: Fix a function that prints instead of returning, so the caller gets None.
debugging.content:
```python
def classify_demand(reading_kw):
    print("OVERLOAD" if reading_kw >= 5000 else "NORMAL")

result = classify_demand(4821)
print(result)  # None
```
Explain exactly why `result` is `None` even though the function clearly printed the correct classification, and fix it.

reflection.summary: Printing and returning are not the same operation.
reflection.content: Why did `result = classify_demand(4821)` end up `None` even though the function clearly printed something correct? Where else might this exact confusion cost you later?

teachBack.summary: Explain return vs. print to someone who insists they're the same.
teachBack.content: Explain the difference between a function returning a value and a function printing a value, to someone who insists they're the same thing because both 'show the answer.'

miniProject.summary: Refactor the sensor-intake project so both call sites share one function.
miniProject.content: Refactor the sensor-intake mini project so the classification logic used in both the single-reading and the batch-loop versions calls one shared function instead of duplicating the if/elif/else chain.

portfolioEvidence.summary: classify_demand() becomes the first named, reusable function in the codebase.
portfolioEvidence.content: classify_demand() becomes the first named, reusable function committed to the Digital Twin's codebase, called from at least two places without duplication.

labs.title: Extract the Duplicate
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise providing intentionally duplicated classification logic across two call sites; the learner must refactor both into calls to a single shared function without changing behavior.

simulations.title: Function Extraction Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows duplicated logic blocks collapsing into a single function block with call-site arrows, live-updating both call sites when the shared logic changes.

flashcards.front: Why did result = classify_demand(4821) come back as None if the function used print() instead of return?
flashcards.back: print() only displays a value to the console; it doesn't hand anything back to the code that called the function. Without an explicit return, a function implicitly returns None.

flashcards.front: What problem does giving a parameter a default value, like safe_limit_kw=5000, solve?
flashcards.back: It lets the common case omit the argument entirely while still allowing callers who need a different limit to override it explicitly.

rubric: Refactor removes the duplication entirely rather than leaving one call site still inline
Function signature (parameter names, defaults) is chosen deliberately, not copied without consideration of what should be a default vs. required
Return values are used correctly throughout — no accidental reliance on print() where a value needs to flow back to the caller
